items.creator: @StuLoren
items.title:
RT by @BalloPeat: Appreciate @susanamendoza10's focus on Chicago's fiscal issues. As for this $1.5b bond issue, a few key points alderman should seek clarity on:

1) Calculations for refi savings. Since deal was floated in mid-Sept, our borrowing rates have materially risen (see below charts). 

Over the last few weeks as markets have reacted to better than anticipated economic data and a rethink on the pace and depth of Fed rate cuts, Chicago's BBB muni curve is up about 30 bps on the long-end (left chart), while the AA revenue bond curve (for the STSC bonds) is up ~5-15 bps depending on tenor (right chart). It strikes me as implausible that the refi/tender savings figures referenced by the city's CFO are still the same. 

To be sure, savings are important - especially when we are dealing with substantial deficits. But we should have more clarity on the size of such savings. De minimis savings may not be worth utilizing the city's balance sheet capacity. Again, any borrowing today crowds out borrowing potential in the future. And borrowing that finances growth-generative investments is qualitatively superior to borrowing that extends the city's debt maturity profile but leaves the balance sheet just as strained.

2) What mechanisms exist to prevent the city at a later date from using any of the borrowing authorization to cover city operating expenses. Yes, we had a change to the language in the offering document restricting proceeds to refinancing, but (a) city government money is fungible and (b) the mayor's statement on the offering yesterday alluded to amendments in the future that could authorize borrowing proceeds for city operating expenses. 

What happens if in a year from now, we have only borrowed the initial $980m and the additional $520m borrowing authority exists while we face an even larger budget deficit. What if the mayor says either amend the terms to allow for borrowing to go toward city operating expenses or we will have to raise property taxes. It is entirely possible the city in that case amends the terms and taps the remaining borrowing capacity for the budget. That would be helpful for a year on the tax front, but leave us in a worse credit situation long-term.

3) Has the city interacted at all with credit agencies to get a feel for how this borrowing will impact our rating. As a reminder, Chicago is the lowest rated large city at BBB. We don't want to risk a negative credit outlook revision or a downgrade to non-investment grade status. That would result in more expensive future borrowing and make our fiscal and tax outlook even more dreadful.

Reading between the lines, credit analysts are not keen on this $1.5b deal. See the statetment from Municipal Market Analytics from a fairly critical article in the industry publication Bond Buyer yesterday:

In a credit recommendation released Tuesday, Municipal Market Analytics said the planned refinancing "highlights the city's diminished financial flexibility within the current and upcoming budget cycles."
            
"Depending on how the refunding is structured — i.e., just how far current principal payments are delayed — this sale could be another signal that the city's decade-long uptrend in credit quality has come to an end," MMA said, adding if the city cannot find new revenues, make meaningful spending cuts or secure help from the state, "Chicago's credit profile and perhaps ratings may have begun to shift downward."
            
4) Why is the city so steadfast about maintaining the current $5/bond underwriting structure? That is well above comp deals, including those with the state last month. When the state issued $1.08b via RBC (the lead underwriter for the city's proposed offering), the pricing was $3.22/bond. That was also for a refinancing. The state issuance was rated A-, which is close enough to the city's BBB rating and STSC bonds AA- ratings, as to not account for such a material difference in price.

What does account for the material price difference seems to be the city paying each of 8 co-manager banks fees of $201,875, and a senior co-manager bank $760,000. Ten different firms involved in the sales/underwriting process seems excessive. While this is not that material relative to the size of the overall borrowing, if the city is in a tight financial spot  it should not be engaging in what appears to be patronage payments as part of this offering.

5) In general, just demand much more information and transparency. Clarity, process and organization are not hallmarks of the Johnson administration. The city essentially is saying "trust us." They have to earn the right. Hold them accountable.
items.dc:creator: @StuLoren
items.content:
Appreciate @susanamendoza10's focus on Chicago's fiscal issues. As for this $1.5b bond issue, a few key points alderman should seek clarity on:

1) Calculations for refi savings. Since deal was floated in mid-Sept, our borrowing rates have materially risen (see below charts).

Over the last few weeks as markets have reacted to better than anticipated economic data and a rethink on the pace and depth of Fed rate cuts, Chicago's BBB muni curve is up about 30 bps on the long-end (left chart), while the AA revenue bond curve (for the STSC bonds) is up ~5-15 bps depending on tenor (right chart). It strikes me as implausible that the refi/tender savings figures referenced by the city's CFO are still the same.

To be sure, savings are important - especially when we are dealing with substantial deficits. But we should have more clarity on the size of such savings. De minimis savings may not be worth utilizing the city's balance sheet capacity. Again, any borrowing today crowds out borrowing potential in the future. And borrowing that finances growth-generative investments is qualitatively superior to borrowing that extends the city's debt maturity profile but leaves the balance sheet just as strained.

2) What mechanisms exist to prevent the city at a later date from using any of the borrowing authorization to cover city operating expenses. Yes, we had a change to the language in the offering document restricting proceeds to refinancing, but (a) city government money is fungible and (b) the mayor's statement on the offering yesterday alluded to amendments in the future that could authorize borrowing proceeds for city operating expenses.

What happens if in a year from now, we have only borrowed the initial $980m and the additional $520m borrowing authority exists while we face an even larger budget deficit. What if the mayor says either amend the terms to allow for borrowing to go toward city operating expenses or we will have to raise property taxes. It is entirely possible the city in that case amends the terms and taps the remaining borrowing capacity for the budget. That would be helpful for a year on the tax front, but leave us in a worse credit situation long-term.

3) Has the city interacted at all with credit agencies to get a feel for how this borrowing will impact our rating. As a reminder, Chicago is the lowest rated large city at BBB. We don't want to risk a negative credit outlook revision or a downgrade to non-investment grade status. That would result in more expensive future borrowing and make our fiscal and tax outlook even more dreadful.

Reading between the lines, credit analysts are not keen on this $1.5b deal. See the statetment from Municipal Market Analytics from a fairly critical article in the industry publication Bond Buyer yesterday:

In a credit recommendation released Tuesday, Municipal Market Analytics said the planned refinancing "highlights the city's diminished financial flexibility within the current and upcoming budget cycles."

"Depending on how the refunding is structured — i.e., just how far current principal payments are delayed — this sale could be another signal that the city's decade-long uptrend in credit quality has come to an end," MMA said, adding if the city cannot find new revenues, make meaningful spending cuts or secure help from the state, "Chicago's credit profile and perhaps ratings may have begun to shift downward."

4) Why is the city so steadfast about maintaining the current $5/bond underwriting structure? That is well above comp deals, including those with the state last month. When the state issued $1.08b via RBC (the lead underwriter for the city's proposed offering), the pricing was $3.22/bond. That was also for a refinancing. The state issuance was rated A-, which is close enough to the city's BBB rating and STSC bonds AA- ratings, as to not account for such a material difference in price.

What does account for the material price difference seems to be the city paying each of 8 co-manager banks fees of $201,875, and a senior co-manager bank $760,000. Ten different firms involved in the sales/underwriting process seems excessive. While this is not that material relative to the size of the overall borrowing, if the city is in a tight financial spot it should not be engaging in what appears to be patronage payments as part of this offering.

5) In general, just demand much more information and transparency. Clarity, process and organization are not hallmarks of the Johnson administration. The city essentially is saying "trust us." They have to earn the right. Hold them accountable.
